item.title: Gambling Games Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers who have completed the gambling basics lesson.

Learning goal: classify gambling games by their mechanical structure and identify what each structure rewards.

Gambling games fall into a few structural families defined by how much the player’s decisions affect the outcome. Understanding which family a game belongs to tells you what kind of thinking it rewards — and where the house makes its money.

Pure chance games give the player no meaningful decisions after the wager is placed. Roulette, slot machines, lottery draws, and craps (on most bets) fall into this category. The player chooses how much to bet and where, but cannot influence the resolution. Expected value is determined entirely by the game’s mathematical structure, and no strategy can change it. These games reward bankroll management and the discipline to stop — nothing else.

item.title: Genealogy of Gambling Games
item.description:
Gambling games inherit from two distinct lineages that converged: games of chance played for entertainment and wagering practices attached to non-game events. The games we recognize today sit at the intersection.

The chance-game lineage. Dice, knucklebones, and drawing lots produced structured uncertain outcomes. These were played socially before anyone attached stakes to them. Over centuries, the instruments became more standardized (fair dice, shuffled cards, balanced wheels) and the rules more formal. The design pressure was toward games that felt fair and produced clear winners — prerequisites for wagering but not dependent on it.

item.title: History of Gambling Games
item.description:
The history of gambling games tracks a long-running interaction between game design, mathematical understanding, and regulatory pressure. New game forms emerge, attract players, provoke regulation, and adapt.

Dice games are the oldest documented gambling games. Hazard, the ancestor of modern craps, was played in England by the thirteenth century and was popular enough to appear in Chaucer. Its rules were complex enough to create meaningful betting decisions despite being a pure chance game — the “caster” chose a “main” number, and side bets proliferated. Craps emerged as a simplified American variant in the nineteenth century, and its migration into casinos formalized the layout and odds structure still used today.
